The presence of incorrect labels in the training dataset violates the assumption that training data is sampled from the true feature-label joint distribution, leading to the lack of performance guarantees based on generalization bounds.

The presence of incorrect labels in the training dataset violates the assumption that training data is sampled from the true feature-label joint distribution, leading to the lack of performance guarantees based on generalization bounds.
Learning algorithms have been introduced that can cope with datasets with incorrect labels while guaranteeing desirable performance through provable generalization bounds.
Solutions to learning from datasets with noisy labels rely on precise knowledge of the error rate associated with the labels.

The iterative noise estimation heuristic proposed by [28] attempts to partly correct the error in noise estimation heuristics. They pointed out errors in computing anchor points in methods introduced by [16] and [27].
The noise estimation heuristic proposed in [28] identifies errors in noise estimation methods and provides conditions under which these methods work or fail.
The methods introduced by [16] and [27] were found to have errors in computing anchor points as pointed out in the context.
In their solution, [26] proposes inferring the transition matrix without needing anchor points. They use instances with the highest class posterior probabilities for noisy data as anchor points.

While most mentioned works do not study the generalization properties of their proposed models, the work in the context addresses this problem and establishes bounds that depend on the estimated noise transition matrix.
